text: The lagna and its lord are the foundation of the chart; a strong lagna lord in a kendra or trikona protects health and gives self-direction (Brihat Parashara Hora Shastra).
The Moon governs manas (the mind); its nakshatra at birth (janma nakshatra) sets the emotional temperament and is the seed of the Vimshottari dasha sequence.
Jupiter in a kendra from the Moon forms Gajakesari yoga, granting wisdom, fame and lasting wealth; the strength of Jupiter decides how fully it fructifies.
Saturn well placed in the 10th or 11th gives slow, durable rise through service and persistence; afflicted Saturn delays but rarely denies what is earned.
The Sun in the 10th house gives authority, government favour and visible achievement; its dignity shows whether power comes with grace or friction.
Venus, karaka of marriage, gives a loving spouse when dignified; combust or debilitated Venus asks for patience and care in relationships.
Mars in the 1st, 2nd, 4th, 7th, 8th or 12th constitutes kuja dosha for marriage; it is cancelled or softened when Mars is in own sign, exalted, or the partner shares the dosha.
Mercury with the Sun forms Budhaditya yoga giving intelligence and skill in speech, provided Mercury is not too close (combust) to the Sun.
Rahu gives worldly amplification: in upachaya houses (3, 6, 10, 11) it grants material success, foreign connections and unconventional rises.
Ketu in the 12th or with the lagna inclines to moksha marga — research, meditation and detachment; material questions matter less to such a native.
The 5th house shows purva punya (past merit), intelligence and children; benefics here bless studies and progeny, malefics ask for remedial devotion.
The 9th is the house of bhagya (fortune); its lord strong and connected to the lagna or 10th makes the native fortunate and guided by dharma.
Planets deliver their promise during their dasha and bhukti; a yoga in the chart waits for the period of its makers to bear fruit (Phaladeepika).
Guru mahadasha, sixteen years, is counted the most benevolent: expansion of family, learning, wealth and spiritual life, in proportion to Jupiter's strength.
Shani mahadasha, nineteen years, rewards honest labour and simple living; it removes what is unearned and consolidates what is deserved.
Rahu mahadasha brings eighteen years of ambition, travel and sudden turns; grounding practices and truthfulness keep its gains stable.
Shukra mahadasha, twenty years, favours marriage, comforts, arts and vehicles when Venus is well placed.
The Moon in Cancer, its own sign, gives emotional strength, care for family and popularity with the public.
An exalted planet acts like a minister given full authority — its significations flower with ease; a debilitated one is a capable worker in the wrong post, needing effort and its dispositor's help.
Association of kendra and trikona lords creates rajayoga — power and prosperity — strongest when it involves the 9th and 10th lords.
The 2nd house governs accumulated wealth, food and speech; Jupiter or Venus here give sweet speech and savings, malefics give blunt words and spending.
All planets tend to give material gains in the 11th house; even malefics become productive here, though the means differ.
The 8th house rules longevity, inheritance and hidden knowledge; strong benefics here give research ability and unexpected legacies.
Malefics in the 6th destroy enemies and give competitive victory; the 6th lord's condition shows health, debts and litigation.
Kemadruma yoga — the Moon unattended by planets on either side — brings phases of loneliness; it is cancelled when the Moon is in a kendra or aspected by Jupiter.
The navamsa (D9) refines the rasi promise, especially for marriage; a planet strong in both rasi and navamsa (vargottama) gives firm results.
Classical remedies are graded: mantra and charity first, fasting on the graha's day next, gemstones only when the planet is a functional benefic.
In Odisha tradition, seva and darshan of Lord Jagannath, offering of anna-bhoga, and observing Ekadashi are considered universal pacifiers of planetary affliction.
For Shani afflictions, Saturday oil-lamp offerings at Shani shrines and service to elders and workers are prescribed; in Odisha, worship at Shani temples on Sanibara is customary.
For Mangal dosha, Hanuman upasana on Tuesdays, recitation of Hanuman Chalisa and red-lentil charity are the traditional first remedies.
Ekadashi tithi is held sacred for fasting and Vishnu worship; work begun on Amavasya is traditionally avoided, while Shukla Panchami and Dashami favour new beginnings.
The Odia calendar is solar: months change at Sankranti (the Sun entering a new rashi). Pana Sankranti (Mesha) opens the Odia new year; Makara, Raja and Dhanu Sankranti are major observances.
Raja Parba, celebrated as Mithuna Sankranti approaches, honours the Earth's womanhood; auspicious for family harmony and the wellbeing of daughters.
Weekday lords colour undertakings: Wednesday and Thursday suit study and commerce, Friday suits marriage and arts, Tuesday is avoided for journeys, Saturday for new ventures.
The namakarana samskara chooses the first syllable of a child's name from the janma-nakshatra pada, binding the name to the Moon's star and harmonising the child with its dasha lords.
In ashtakoota, gana koota compares temperaments — deva (sattvic), manushya (rajasic), rakshasa (tamasic); deva–rakshasa unions need other kootas strong to compensate.

Nadi koota carries the highest weight (8 points) as it concerns progeny and health compatibility; same-nadi (nadi dosha) is traditionally excused when rashi lords are friends or nakshatra padas differ.
Bhakoot dosha (2/12, 5/9, 6/8 rashi positions) affects prosperity and family growth; friendship between the two rashi lords is the classical cancellation.
In Samudrika Shastra the heart line shows the flow of affection: curving to Jupiter's mount promises idealistic, generous love; a straight line shows measured devotion.
The head line sloping to the Moon's mount marks imagination; straight across the palm marks calculation — both are gifts when the thumb shows will.
The life line's sweep shows vitality, not lifespan; a sister line beside it is the mark of protection (raksha rekha).
